master_framework_metadata.framework_description: Continuously updated master investing framework distilled from Warren Buffett shareholder letters.

framework_categories.category_description: Business quality and industry economics principles

principles.principle_title: Industry structure can overwhelm managerial effort
principles.principle_statement: Business quality depends heavily on industry structure; even capable managers may earn poor returns when structural economics are hostile and improvements are easily copied.
principles.normalized_principle: Do not confuse managerial effort with favorable economics: industry tailwinds and headwinds strongly shape long-term returns.
metric_definition: return_on_capital_employed: return earned by the business despite management effort
industry_profitability: the level of profitability available to participants in the industry
incremental_return_on_capital: the payoff from additional managerial or capital improvements
industry_return_on_equity: industry-wide returns earned by participants over time
margin_persistence: durability of margins across competitors and cycles
capital_intensity: the amount of capital the industry requires to compete
pricing_power: ability of firms in the industry to sustain prices and returns
metric_usage_context: return_on_capital_employed: test whether good managers are operating in a bad industry
industry_profitability: determine whether the structure rewards excellence or suppresses returns broadly
incremental_return_on_capital: assess whether improvements are likely to earn durable value or be competed away
industry_return_on_equity: use it to judge whether low returns are structural rather than company-specific
pricing_power: use it to assess whether the industry supports attractive economics for any manager
metric_limitations: managerial_reputation: can overstate business attractiveness if industry structure overwhelms effort
incremental_return_on_capital: may stay poor even with strong execution if competitors copy improvements
management_performance: judging managers in isolation can mislead when the whole industry chronically earns poor returns
margin_persistence: cyclical or structurally weak industries may punish even capable operators
performance_logic: Favorable industries can allow good businesses to prosper despite mistakes, while structurally weak industries can suppress returns even under able managers.
Industry economics often determine whether execution can translate into attractive returns.
In difficult industries, operational gains are often transient because competitors pursue the same obvious fixes.
A bad industry can absorb intelligence and effort while still producing poor returns.
A bad industry can neutralize talented management, while a favorable industry can make ordinary management look better; the investment decision must account for structural industry economics.
Underlying business economics set the ceiling for what management can achieve.
Turnaround stories that rely primarily on managerial brilliance often ignore structural constraints.
A business with favorable economics should still look attractive under merely competent management.
evaluation_questions: Is the business pushed forward by favorable industry forces or backward by structural disadvantages?
Can good execution translate into strong returns in this industry?
Are improving results driven by durable industry economics or temporary conditions?
Is the manager fighting a structurally hard business?
Are improvements proprietary, or available to every competitor?
Does the industry reward excellence, or punish everyone equally?
Are low returns caused by management weakness or by the economics of the industry?
Is this an industry where good outcomes are structurally easy or hard?
Are competitors also earning poor returns despite effort?
Is the thesis relying on management brilliance to defeat poor industry economics?
Are underlying economics inherently favorable, or only temporarily masked?
Would average competent management still have a good business here?
diagnostic_signals: Industry-wide low margins and weak returns on capital.
A business requires exceptional effort just to achieve mediocre results.
Management stories overshadow poor structural economics.
Capable management paired with persistently weak economics
Industry-wide low returns despite broad operational effort
Improvements that are easy for rivals to match
Chronic low returns across competitors
High capital intensity and weak pricing power
Good managers unable to produce strong economics in the sector
The investment case depends heavily on charismatic management narratives.
Industry or business-model economics remain poor despite management praise.
Operational fixes are expected to solve structural economic problems.
practical_rules: Give heavy weight to industry structure when assessing quality.
Do not confuse managerial effort with durable economic advantage.
Separate admiration for management from judgment on industry economics.
Do not assume excellence can overcome structurally poor industry conditions.
Value proprietary advantages more than generic operational effort.
Diagnose industry structure before concluding management is weak.
Avoid expecting management heroics to overcome persistently poor industry economics.
Put more weight on business economics than on managerial storytelling.
Avoid turnarounds that depend mainly on heroic execution without structural advantage.
Ask whether the business would still be good under average competent management.
example_buffett_phrasing: tailwinds vs. headwinds
put greater weight on business quality and industry structure than on heroic operational stories
Industry structure can overwhelm managerial effort
not a reflection on managers, but on the industry
industry economics matter more than management effort
when brilliant management meets a bad business, the business reputation usually remains intact

principles.principle_title: Prefer error-tolerant businesses
principles.principle_statement: Superior businesses remain attractive despite normal managerial mistakes, while weak businesses require near-perfect execution merely to earn modest returns.
principles.normalized_principle: Prefer businesses whose economics are robust enough to tolerate ordinary human error.
performance_logic: Robust economics provide a margin of safety against inevitable mistakes.
Fragile businesses magnify small errors into poor returns because the base economics are weak.
evaluation_questions: If management makes a few mistakes, does the business still work?
How fragile are margins, demand, or capital allocation to small errors?
Does this business need perfection to earn acceptable returns?
diagnostic_signals: Minor execution errors quickly destroy profitability.
The business lacks margin structure or competitive resilience.
Good managers still produce only mediocre returns.
practical_rules: Favor businesses that can survive normal mistakes.
Avoid businesses that require near-perfect execution to be merely adequate.
example_buffett_phrasing: mistakes are survivable
perfection still yields mediocre returns

principles.principle_title: Growth is best when it requires little incremental capital
principles.principle_statement: Growth is most valuable when earnings can expand without proportional increases in invested capital and when incremental returns remain high.
principles.normalized_principle: Capital-light growth creates superior compounding because cash is freed for redeployment instead of being consumed by the business just to grow.
performance_logic: Capital-light growth frees cash for distribution or redeployment into other high-return uses.
Low incremental capital requirements often indicate economic goodwill, pricing power, or franchise strength.
Cash-generative businesses increase financial flexibility as they grow.
Capital-hungry businesses can show growth while weakening owner economics because all internally generated funds are absorbed by the business.
A business that must consume its cash merely to maintain position has mirage-like economics.
evaluation_questions: How much additional capital was required to produce recent growth?
Can earnings expand without continuous heavy reinvestment?
Are growth opportunities internally funded and high-return?
Does this business generate surplus cash after maintenance needs?
Is management forced to reinvest everything merely to maintain physical volume?
Does the company strengthen financial flexibility as it grows, or reduce it?
diagnostic_signals: Revenue and earnings growth consume large amounts of additional capital.
Maintenance and growth capex absorb most operating cash.
Incremental returns on new capital are weak.
Growth absorbs all internally generated cash.
The business needs continual reinvestment just to maintain position.
Financial flexibility does not improve despite reported success.
practical_rules: Prioritize businesses with low incremental capital needs.
Measure growth quality by return on incremental invested capital, not by growth alone.
Favor self-financing growth over capital-hungry growth.
Assess whether the business strengthens or weakens financial flexibility over time.
Treat persistent cash consumption as a business-quality issue, not just a financing issue.
example_buffett_phrasing: grow without requiring proportional capital
growth is valuable only when the capital required is sensible relative to the return produced
generate cash, not those that consume it

sub_principles.sub_principle_title: Favor cash earnings on modest capital employed
sub_principles.sub_principle_statement: Favor businesses that produce very large amounts relative to capital employed, especially when earnings show up in cash rather than being absorbed into receivables or inventory.
sub_principles.normalized_sub_principle: Businesses are superior when they generate high cash earnings on capital and do not need heavy working-capital absorption to realize profits.
sub_principles.primary_analytical_question: Do profits convert into cash without being absorbed by working capital or tangible capital needs?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when testing whether earnings show up as cash and whether growth consumes receivables, inventory, or tangible capital.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when the question is only whether earnings can grow with low incremental capital; use the parent capital-light growth principle.

performance_logic: High returns are most valuable when they arrive in cash and require little reinvestment; working-capital-heavy growth can consume capital and reduce the owner's actual yield.
Accounting earnings matter only if they ultimately release cash that owners can use.
Businesses that consume cash merely to maintain operations have weaker economics than reported profits suggest.
Strong cash conversion indicates that reported earnings correspond to real owner benefit.

earnings_growth_vs_cash_growth: the comparison between growth in accounting earnings and growth in cash generation

cash_from_operations_minus_maintenance_capex: used to separate distributable cash from gross operating cash flow
cash_conversion_ratio: used to flag businesses whose earnings quality is weak
earnings_growth_vs_cash_growth: used to test whether profit growth is real or only accounting-deep

free_cash_flow_conversion: weak conversion suggests lower owner value than accounting profits imply

principles.principle_title: Avoid commodity economics plus high capital intensity
principles.principle_statement: Businesses with undifferentiated products, weak pricing power, excess capacity, low margins, and heavy capital needs usually earn inadequate returns on capital.
principles.normalized_principle: High capital requirements combined with weak differentiation and poor industry structure usually destroy returns on capital.

capital_turnover: revenue generated per dollar of capital employed
working_capital_intensity: receivables and inventory required per dollar of sales
operating_margin: operating profit earned per dollar of revenue
incremental_return_on_capital: additional earnings generated by each additional unit of capital invested

cheap_asset_base: old or cheap assets do not rescue a business whose economics remain weak
performance_logic: Low margins combined with low capital turnover produce poor returns on capital.
When products are commodity-like and improvements are easily copied, superior economics are hard to sustain.
Heavy working capital and fixed assets trap more capital in a business that cannot earn enough on it.
evaluation_questions: Is the product differentiated or essentially a commodity?
Does industry supply exceed demand most of the time?
Do prices reflect full capital costs, or merely direct operating costs?
How much receivables, inventory, plant, and equipment are required per dollar of sales?
Can any operational improvement be competed away quickly?

practical_rules: Do not judge a business on profit margin alone; connect margin to turnover and capital needs.
Avoid treating cheap assets as evidence of good economics.
Prioritize businesses that combine differentiation with attractive returns on required capital.
example_buffett_phrasing: High capital requirements plus weak differentiation usually lead to poor returns

principles.principle_title: A slow-growth industry can still contain a great business
principles.principle_statement: Industry volume growth is not the same as business quality; an excellent company can exist in a slow-growth industry if it has durable economics, pricing power, loyalty, or low capital needs.
principles.normalized_principle: A slow-growth industry can still contain an excellent business when company-level economics are durable and capital-light.
performance_logic: Business value can come from pricing power, customer attachment, and efficient economics rather than industry volume growth.
Low-growth industries may hide strong unit economics and cash generation.
evaluation_questions: Is this company creating superior economics despite limited industry growth?
Are margins, pricing, or loyalty driving value more than unit growth?
Is industry stagnation obscuring business quality?
diagnostic_signals: Analysts dismiss the business mainly because industry growth is low.
The company still produces strong earnings growth or cash flow with little added capital.
practical_rules: Do not reject a business solely because its industry lacks volume growth.
Favor durable economics over fashionable growth narratives.
example_buffett_phrasing: a no-growth industry can still hold a great business
favor durable economics over fashionable growth stories

principles.principle_title: Hold mediocre businesses only within clear boundaries
principles.principle_statement: A mediocre business can be worth holding, but not buying or heavily reinvesting in, when it is unlikely to destroy cash, has honest energetic managers, and has strategic or human considerations.
principles.normalized_principle: Distinguish a tolerable holding from an attractive reinvestment vehicle; do not confuse acceptable ownership with a good buying thesis.
metric_definition: cash_return: the cash the business generates over time
cash_losses: recurring negative cash output from operations or required support
reinvestment_return: return available from incremental capital put into the business
metric_usage_context: cash_return: determine whether the business at least earns modest cash over time
cash_losses: set the boundary between tolerable mediocrity and value destruction
reinvestment_return: distinguish a tolerable holding from an attractive place to allocate new capital
metric_limitations: cash_return: modest positive cash generation does not make a weak business a great investment
reinvestment_return: poor reinvestment prospects mean the holding logic should not be mistaken for a buying thesis
performance_logic: A business may justify continued ownership if it does not consume cash destructively and management quality plus non-financial factors matter.
This logic is about preserving acceptable economics and relationships, not maximizing capital efficiency.
evaluation_questions: Is the business merely mediocre, or actually value-destructive?
Does it at least earn cash over time?
Are there strategic, reputational, or human reasons to continue ownership?
Is management candid enough to justify patient support?
diagnostic_signals: Weak reinvestment prospects but low likelihood of cash losses
Honest, energetic managers in a structurally challenged business
Community or reputational considerations tied to ownership
practical_rules: Treat this as a holding principle, not a buying principle.
Continue support only when cash destruction is unlikely and management quality is high.
Do not mistake tolerable ownership for attractive reinvestment.
example_buffett_phrasing: A business can be worth holding even if it is not an ideal reinvestment vehicle

framework_categories.category_description: Durable competitive advantage, hard-to-duplicate economics, customer value, and competition principles

principles.principle_title: Seek hard-to-duplicate competitive advantages
principles.principle_statement: A superior business has structural advantages competitors cannot easily replicate, allowing it to deliver unusual customer value while sustaining unusual returns.
principles.normalized_principle: Durable superior returns require a hard-to-duplicate advantage rather than a temporary operational lead.
principles.primary_analytical_question: What hard-to-duplicate advantage allows this business to sustain superior returns?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when assessing moat, customer captivity, cost advantage, durability of returns, competitor imitation, or whether superior economics can persist.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when the issue is industry-wide returns regardless of company moat; use business_quality_001. Do not retrieve for capital-light growth mechanics alone; use business_quality_003.

performance_logic: Hard-to-duplicate advantages let a company offer better customer value while earning superior returns.
Structural advantages are more durable than promotional or managerial edges.
Durable competitive advantage supports long-term compounding and protects returns from imitation.

customer_retention: the persistence of customer relationships over time
industry_return_persistence: the durability of excess returns within an industry or business
market_share_stability: the ability to maintain share over time despite competition
margin_durability_vs_peers: the persistence of superior margins relative to peers
metric_usage_context: relative_cost_position: used to identify structural cost advantages that support superior value to customers
customer_retention: used to test whether the advantage creates durable customer loyalty
industry_return_persistence: used to judge whether superior economics are durable rather than fleeting
market_share_stability: used to observe competitive resilience
margin_durability_vs_peers: used to assess whether the advantage shows up in sustained economic superiority
metric_limitations: relative_cost_position: can shift if technology or industry structure changes

industry_return_persistence: may be influenced by cyclical conditions rather than structural quality
market_share_stability: can remain steady temporarily even as economics deteriorate
margin_durability_vs_peers: can be distorted by accounting differences or short-term mix shifts
evaluation_questions: What exactly is the hard-to-duplicate advantage?
Does it let the company offer better value while still earning superior returns?
Is the advantage durable under competitive adaptation?
diagnostic_signals: The business maintains superior economics without obvious reliance on temporary promotions.
Competitors struggle to copy the company's value proposition or cost position.
Returns and share remain resilient over time.
practical_rules: Seek structural advantages, not promotional ones.
Look for businesses combining customer value, scalability, and durable economics.
Test whether the advantage is easy or hard for competitors to replicate.

principles.principle_title: Reconstruct economic reality before trusting accounting presentation
principles.principle_statement: Reported accounting is only a starting point; analysis should reconstruct the economic reality by adjusting misleading denominators, ownership attribution, segment economics, book-value relevance, and recurring earning power.
principles.normalized_principle: Judge performance and value by economically meaningful earnings, assets, ownership interests, and capital bases rather than by accounting presentation alone.
metric_definition: segment_earnings: earnings generated by each operating or investment segment viewed separately
ownership_attributable_earnings: the portion of earnings economically attributable to the shareholder's ownership stake
minority_interest: the share of subsidiary economics that belongs to outside owners rather than the parent
reported_consolidated_earnings: aggregate earnings presented under consolidated accounting rules
book_value: accounting value of equity on the balance sheet
return_on_book_capital: earnings earned on book equity
purchase_price_to_book: purchase price relative to book value
metric_usage_context: segment_earnings: separate businesses with different economics instead of judging the enterprise from one blended total
ownership_attributable_earnings: identify what earnings truly belong to shareholders after considering partial ownership
minority_interest: adjust consolidated results so reported profit is not mistaken for wholly owned economics
reported_consolidated_earnings: treat reported totals as a starting point that may hide recurring, investment-related, or accounting-driven differences
book_value: test whether accounting capital is a useful proxy for economic value
return_on_book_capital: determine whether the company earns attractive economics on the capital represented by book
purchase_price_to_book: assess whether a discount or premium to book is justified

segment_earnings: may still mislead if one-time distortions, restatements, or ownership effects are not separated
ownership_attributable_earnings: requires careful attribution and can be obscured by GAAP presentation
book_value: can mislead when assets are not marked in economically meaningful ways or when returns on book are poor
purchase_price_to_book: cheapness to book alone is not attractive if underlying business quality is weak
performance_logic: A ratio is only meaningful if the denominator approximates real capital employed.
Distorted book values or unusual leverage can make unadjusted ROE or similar ratios misstate true economics.
Blended reporting can mask which businesses truly create value and which only affect accounting presentation.
Accurate capital allocation requires knowing what economics are recurring, attributable, and decision-relevant.
A discount to book matters only if the book capital supports attractive economics.
Book value is most useful when asset values and the returns earned on them are economically informative.
evaluation_questions: Are assets carried at unrealistic values?
Does reported equity fairly represent the capital employed in the business?
Would return on tangible equity or return on invested capital be more informative here?
What parts of reported earnings are actually attributable to shareholders?
Which reported numbers combine businesses with very different economics?
Does the financial presentation clarify reality or hide it?
Does book value approximate economic value here?
Is the company earning high returns on book capital?
Is a discount to book justified by poor economics, or is it a genuine bargain?
diagnostic_signals: Large accounting distortions in balance-sheet carrying values.
Unusual leverage makes equity-based ratios swing wildly.
Reported ROE looks strong but underlying capital intensity is still high.
Large differences in ownership percentages across segments
Heavy reliance on consolidated figures without segment economics
One-time accounting distortions or merger restatements affecting reported results
Insurance or asset-heavy businesses where book may matter
Large discount to book without clear explanation
Poor returns on book capital despite low valuation
practical_rules: Adjust the denominator before drawing conclusions from standard ratios.
Prefer the capital measure that best reflects economic reality, not accounting convenience.
Rebuild performance by segment, ownership stake, and economic function.
Separate fully owned, partly owned, operating, investment-related, recurring, and accounting-driven items.
Analyze what management would review internally, not only what GAAP presents externally.
Use book value as context, not as a standalone bargain signal.
Pair price-to-book analysis with return-on-capital analysis.
Ignore book value when it lacks economic meaning.
example_buffett_phrasing: standard ratios are useful only if the denominator is economically meaningful
adjust the framework when accounting figures obscure business reality
Economic reality matters more than accounting presentation
Book value can matter, but only in context

principles.principle_title: Measure earnings against the capital required to produce them
principles.principle_statement: Management and business performance should be judged by returns earned on the capital actually required, not by EPS, profit growth, or earnings in isolation.
principles.normalized_principle: Capital efficiency is the primary scorecard: earnings growth is valuable only when it exceeds the growth in required capital and produces attractive returns.

principles.principle_title: Prefer a good business at a fair price over a bargain bad business
principles.principle_statement: A good business purchased at a fair price is superior to a poor business purchased at a bargain price, and turnarounds seldom turn.

sub_principles.sub_principle_title: Durable easy businesses can justify premium valuations

sub_principles.sub_principle_title: Overpayment can erase business excellence
sub_principles.sub_principle_statement: Even an excellent business can become a poor investment when bought at a foolish price because overpayment pre-spends years of future compounding.

principles.principle_title: Management quality requires owner-oriented integrity, competence, and allocation skill
principles.principle_statement: The best managers combine integrity, operating competence, rational capital allocation, discipline against uneconomic growth, cost consciousness, and long-term owner thinking.

principles.principle_title: Management can be the moat in weakly differentiated businesses
principles.principle_statement: In commoditized or weakly differentiated businesses, exceptional people, judgment, and systems can become a major source of performance dispersion, though key-person durability must be tested.

principles.principle_statement: Insurance float is an advantage only when underwriting discipline keeps its cost low or negative; large float generated through poor pricing is a liability, not an asset.

working_capital_intensity: used to test whether inflation forces more capital into inventories, receivables, or other operating assets
maintenance_capex_burden: used to distinguish businesses that must reinvest heavily just to stand still
real_earnings_growth_per_unit_of_capital: used to judge whether the business truly compounds in real terms
metric_limitations: incremental_capital_per_unit_of_growth: can vary over time and by growth phase
working_capital_intensity: can be distorted by temporary balance-sheet timing effects
maintenance_capex_burden: can be hard to separate cleanly from growth capex
real_earnings_growth_per_unit_of_capital: depends on inflation adjustments and normalized capital measurement
evaluation_questions: Can the business raise prices and earnings without major capital injections?
Does growth require more inventory, receivables, plant, or other capital just to maintain real volume?
Is the business truly indexed, or only appearing to grow through reinvestment?
diagnostic_signals: Revenue and earnings grow, but working capital and fixed asset needs rise proportionally.
Inflation forces continual capital injections just to maintain current operations.
Real owner returns lag nominal earnings growth.
practical_rules: Favor businesses with low incremental capital requirements.
Examine working capital and maintenance capex needs explicitly in inflationary settings.
Treat capital intensity as a central determinant of business quality.
example_buffett_phrasing: without needing more capital, including working capital
capital intensity as hidden enemy

framework_categories.category_description: Fixed-income, duration, nominal-claim, and bond-accounting risk principles.

principles.principle_title: Avoid long-duration nominal claims when inflation threatens real returns
principles.principle_statement: Very long-term fixed-rate bonds are dangerous in inflationary environments because they lock in nominal returns while purchasing power deteriorates.
principles.normalized_principle: Long-duration fixed nominal claims are unattractive when inflation threatens real purchasing power.
principles.primary_analytical_question: Does a fixed-income instrument lock owners into poor real purchasing-power economics?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when analyzing bonds, fixed-rate instruments, duration, inflation exposure, or nominal claims on money.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve for equity business real-return analysis; use real_owner_returns_001.

performance_logic: A fixed claim on money becomes less valuable when currency purchasing power falls; the longer the duration, the more the owner is locked into poor real economics.
metric_definition: duration: sensitivity of a fixed-income instrument to changes in rates and time-locked nominal claims
real_yield: nominal bond return after inflation
inflation_expectations: expected future erosion of currency purchasing power
mark_to_market_loss: decline in bond value as rates rise or real economics worsen
metric_usage_context: duration: use it to evaluate how exposed the instrument is to inflation and fixed nominal pricing of money
real_yield: use it to test whether the bond preserves purchasing power
metric_limitations: real_yield: nominal coupons can mislead when inflation is high
duration: long time locks can create severe economic damage even when accounting treatment softens the appearance
evaluation_questions: Is the instrument fixing nominal returns too far into an inflationary future?
What happens to real purchasing power if inflation persists?
Is accounting treatment hiding economic impairment?
diagnostic_signals: Low or negative real yield
High duration in an inflationary regime
Economic impairment masked by accounting conventions
practical_rules: Be skeptical of long-duration nominal bonds in inflationary periods.
Judge fixed-income securities in real purchasing-power terms, not coupon terms alone.
example_buffett_phrasing: very long-term fixed-rate bonds are dangerous
fixed price on money for decades

sub_principles.sub_principle_title: Accounting can hide economic bond risk
sub_principles.sub_principle_statement: Carrying bonds at amortized cost can delay recognition of economic losses and encourage risk blindness.
sub_principles.normalized_sub_principle: Accounting treatment should not substitute for economic reality when evaluating fixed-income risk.
sub_principles.primary_analytical_question: Is amortized-cost or carrying-value accounting hiding true fixed-income economic losses?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when bond accounting, amortized cost, mark-to-market gaps, or hidden losses may obscure fixed-income risk.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when the main issue is duration/inflation risk itself; use parent fixed_income_risk_001.

performance_logic: When accounting does not force recognition of market value changes, economic deterioration can persist unnoticed, leading decision-makers to underestimate risk.
metric_definition: economic_value_vs_carrying_value: difference between true market economics and accounting book value
mark_to_market_sensitivity: how much value changes when market rates and prices move
hidden_losses: economic losses not yet visible in carrying values
metric_usage_context: economic_value_vs_carrying_value: use it to restate the true economics of bond holdings
hidden_losses: use it to identify risk concealed by accounting convention
metric_limitations: carrying_value: amortized cost can conceal genuine impairment and delay corrective action
hidden_losses: accounting invisibility can encourage management complacency
evaluation_questions: Does accounting treatment conceal genuine economic losses?
What would values look like under mark-to-market conditions?
Is management relying on accounting convention instead of economic thinking?
diagnostic_signals: Large gap between carrying value and market value
Management comfort with book values despite deteriorating economics
practical_rules: Mentally mark fixed-income holdings to economic reality.
Treat accounting stability as potentially misleading when market economics have changed.

source_evidence.principle_title_at_source: Accounting can hide economic bond risk

sub_principles.sub_principle_title: Do not optimize around a structurally bad fixed-income setup
sub_principles.sub_principle_statement: Partial cleverness is not protection against a major adverse tide; tactical refinements do not fix a fundamentally bad asset-class exposure.
sub_principles.normalized_sub_principle: Minor optimizations do not redeem an exposure whose underlying economics are structurally unattractive.
sub_principles.primary_analytical_question: Is clever security selection merely improving a fundamentally unattractive fixed-income exposure?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when evaluating tactical refinements to an asset class with hostile inflation, duration, or real-return economics.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when the fixed-income exposure is structurally sound and the issue is instrument-level optionality; use fixed_income_risk_001_sub_003.

performance_logic: When the underlying environment is hostile, tactical sophistication often only reduces pain rather than creating an attractive investment; avoidance may be superior.
metric_definition: scenario_return: expected performance under favorable and adverse scenarios
inflation_adjusted_return: return after considering inflation and opportunity cost
structural_exposure: remaining core risk after tactical optimization
metric_usage_context: structural_exposure: use it to test whether clever security design actually changes the unfavorable economics
scenario_return: use it to compare outright avoidance against optimized participation
metric_limitations: scenario_return: modest improvements can distract from still-poor base economics
structural_exposure: a refined version of a bad bet can remain a bad bet
evaluation_questions: Am I trying to engineer around a structurally bad exposure?
Is this a true edge or just sophistication layered on a poor underlying bet?
Would outright avoidance be better than clever optimization?
diagnostic_signals: Improved structure but still poor real returns
Optimization that leaves core inflation or duration risk intact
practical_rules: Test whether the overall asset-class setup is attractive before engineering refinements.
Prefer avoidance to cleverness when the structural tide is adverse.

source_evidence.principle_title_at_source: Do not optimize around a structurally bad fixed-income setup

sub_principles.sub_principle_title: Optionality can shorten economic duration
sub_principles.sub_principle_statement: Embedded optionality, such as conversion rights, can make a nominally long-duration fixed-income instrument behave like a shorter one economically.
sub_principles.normalized_sub_principle: Meaningful optionality can reduce duration risk and improve the economics of fixed-income securities.
sub_principles.primary_analytical_question: Does embedded optionality materially reduce duration risk or add upside participation?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when evaluating convertibles, embedded options, effective duration, or equity participation inside fixed-income securities.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when the option is remote or cosmetic; use parent duration-risk principle.

performance_logic: Optionality can change a bond's economic profile by reducing time locked into a fixed claim and adding upside participation if conditions improve.
metric_definition: effective_duration: economic duration after considering embedded options
conversion_value: value of the right to convert into equity or otherwise alter the claim
equity_participation_potential: upside from participating in better business outcomes
downside_protection: retained fixed-income protection if the option is not used
metric_usage_context: effective_duration: use it to evaluate whether optionality materially reduces fixed-claim risk
conversion_value: use it to judge whether the embedded option is real rather than cosmetic
metric_limitations: conversion_value: some options are too remote to materially change economics
effective_duration: nominal maturity can overstate or understate risk depending on the option's relevance
evaluation_questions: Does the security contain a real option that reduces duration risk?
Is the option meaningful or merely cosmetic?
How much of expected return comes from fixed claim versus equity-like upside?
diagnostic_signals: High conversion value relative to bond value
Reduced effective duration versus stated maturity
Meaningful upside without surrendering all downside protection
practical_rules: Favor optionality only when it materially alters economics.
Evaluate fixed-income instruments by effective duration, not stated maturity alone.

framework_categories.category_description: Balance-sheet resilience, leverage, liquidity, and financing-risk principles

principles.principle_title: Preserve balance-sheet strength for resilience and optionality
principles.principle_statement: Financial strength, liquidity, moderate debt, and well-structured obligations create staying power and the ability to act decisively when capital becomes scarce, even if they reduce current headline return on equity.
principles.normalized_principle: Balance-sheet conservatism is valuable because it preserves survival, flexibility, and opportunity capture across stress.
principles.primary_analytical_question: Does the balance sheet preserve survival, flexibility, and opportunity capture across stress?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when evaluating leverage, liquidity, debt maturity, refinancing risk, financial firepower, or ability to act in downturns.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when the issue is fixed-income duration risk; use fixed_income_risk_001. Do not retrieve merely to explain high ROE quality; use accounting_earnings_quality_002_sub_001.

performance_logic: Strong balance sheets let firms endure stress and act when others cannot.
Conservatism may lower current ROE but raises survival odds and opportunity capture.
Optionality has strategic value when capital becomes scarce or industries become distressed.
Fragile capital structures may improve current optics while increasing the risk of forced actions in downturns.
Endurance creates compounding value by preventing permanent capital impairment and preserving opportunity capture.
Comfort and flexibility are strategic assets when shocks occur.
metric_definition: liquidity_ratio: liquid resources relative to near-term obligations or needs
debt_maturity_structure: the timing and composition of debt obligations over time
fixed_charge_coverage: the capacity of earnings or cash flow to cover fixed financing obligations
debt_to_equity: debt capital relative to equity capital
excess_capital_relative_to_operating_obligations: capital available beyond what is required to meet operating commitments
metric_usage_context: liquidity_ratio: used to judge immediate financial flexibility and staying power
debt_maturity_structure: used to assess whether liabilities are properly structured for the business's cash flows
fixed_charge_coverage: used to test resilience under stress
debt_to_equity: used to evaluate whether leverage remains moderate
excess_capital_relative_to_operating_obligations: used to gauge financial firepower for future opportunities
metric_limitations: liquidity_ratio: can overstate safety if liquid assets are encumbered or needed for operations
debt_maturity_structure: can look acceptable until refinancing markets tighten
fixed_charge_coverage: can deteriorate quickly in cyclical downturns
debt_to_equity: can mislead when asset values or equity are distorted by accounting
excess_capital_relative_to_operating_obligations: requires judgment about true contingent obligations
evaluation_questions: Does the company have the capital strength to act when others cannot?
Is debt moderate and properly structured?
Is management sacrificing long-term safety and optionality for a slightly higher current ROE?
Can the company survive a severe downturn without forced actions?
Does debt structure match asset characteristics and cash flows?
Is management optimizing for optics, or for durable compounding?
diagnostic_signals: Management optimizes for current ROE at the expense of liquidity and structure.
Debt structure mismatches the business's asset characteristics or cash flows.
The company lacks dry powder during stress.
Leverage is set to maximize headline ROE rather than resilience.
Refinancing risk or covenant pressure could force poor decisions in stress.
The business lacks comfort and flexibility despite acceptable current profitability.
practical_rules: Judge leverage by both cost and the staying power it preserves or destroys.
Give credit for conservatism that increases resilience and decision freedom.
Prefer liquidity, moderate debt, and proper structure over maximized headline ROE.
Penalize fragile balance sheets even when near-term profitability looks better.
Give credit for conservatism that increases durability.
Treat survival and optionality as explicit capital structure objectives.
example_buffett_phrasing: financial firepower
financial strength and flexibility are strategic assets
optionality through conservatism
survival, optionality, and comfort
maximum ROE is not the objective; intelligent endurance is

source_evidence.principle_title_at_source: Balance-sheet strength as strategic optionality
Endurance-oriented capital structure

merge_history.notes: Created new risk-and-leverage principle because balance-sheet optionality is not reducible to ROE quality or fixed-income duration risk.
Extended balance-sheet optionality principle with explicit survival-over-headline-ROE capital-structure logic.

framework_categories.category_description: Organizational design principles covering decentralization, autonomy, and control trade-offs.

principles.principle_title: Centralize capital allocation and decentralize operations
principles.principle_statement: Financial decisions should be centralized while operating authority is heavily delegated to business-unit managers.
principles.normalized_principle: Capital allocation and operations should be organized separately, with central financial discipline and decentralized operating autonomy.
principles.primary_analytical_question: Does the organization separate central capital discipline from decentralized operating autonomy?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when evaluating conglomerates, holding companies, headquarters design, decentralization, or operator autonomy.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when judging individual management candor or capital allocation returns without organizational-design implications.

performance_logic: Operating excellence and capital allocation are different skills; combining central capital judgment with local operating autonomy can improve incentives, speed, and talent attraction.
metric_definition: hq_cost_ratio: corporate overhead relative to the size or profitability of the enterprise
manager_retention: persistence of high-quality operating leaders within the system
decision_speed: speed with which operating decisions are made
capital_allocation_track_record: quality of central decisions about deploying capital
metric_usage_context: hq_cost_ratio: use it to judge whether headquarters adds value or bureaucracy
manager_retention: use it to assess whether decentralized autonomy attracts and keeps strong operators
capital_allocation_track_record: use it to judge whether centralized financial control is skilled
metric_limitations: centralization: too much central control can slow decisions and repel operators
decentralization: autonomy can permit occasional mistakes if financial oversight is weak
evaluation_questions: Who makes capital allocation decisions, and are they excellent at it?
Are operators given enough autonomy to act like owners?
Is corporate overhead adding value or just layers?
diagnostic_signals: Low headquarters burden
Strong subsidiary manager retention
Fast decentralized decisions paired with disciplined capital deployment
practical_rules: Centralize capital allocation expertise.
Delegate operations broadly to capable managers rather than building bureaucracy.
example_buffett_phrasing: financial decisions centralized at the top
operating authority delegated heavily

sub_principles.sub_principle_title: Owner-like autonomy attracts great managers
sub_principles.sub_principle_statement: Extraordinary managers are attracted when working for the company feels almost identical to running their own show.
sub_principles.normalized_sub_principle: Owner-like autonomy is a competitive advantage in recruiting and retaining high-agency managers.
sub_principles.primary_analytical_question: Does the organization use autonomy as a talent magnet for high-agency managers?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when assessing whether an organization attracts and retains exceptional operators through autonomy and ownership-like responsibility.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when testing capital-allocation centralization itself; use organizational_design_001.

performance_logic: Talented operators prefer environments where judgment, responsibility, and freedom resemble ownership; organizational design can therefore become a source of managerial edge.
metric_definition: manager_tenure: duration for which capable operators remain in leadership roles
incentive_alignment: extent to which managers are rewarded like owners
decentralization_level: practical degree of operating autonomy granted to managers
metric_usage_context: manager_tenure: use it to see whether the system retains entrepreneurial operators
incentive_alignment: use it to judge whether autonomy is paired with owner-like behavior
metric_limitations: decentralization_level: nominal autonomy may be meaningless if bureaucracy still dominates
incentive_alignment: poor incentives can undermine owner-like conduct despite autonomy
evaluation_questions: Does the structure attract high-agency managers?
Are incentives and autonomy sufficient for owner-like behavior?
Is bureaucracy repelling the very people the company most needs?
diagnostic_signals: Long tenure of entrepreneurial subsidiary leaders
Lean oversight with strong accountability
Bureaucratic systems that repel exceptional operators
practical_rules: Use autonomy as a talent magnet.
Design the system so outstanding managers can operate as if they own the business.

source_evidence.principle_title_at_source: Great managers value autonomy like ownership

sub_principles.sub_principle_title: Some mistakes are worth the benefits of decentralization
sub_principles.sub_principle_statement: Occasional major mistakes are acceptable if the system is lower-cost, faster, and better at attracting talent overall.
sub_principles.normalized_sub_principle: A zero-mistake organization may destroy more value through cost and bureaucracy than it saves through tighter control.
sub_principles.primary_analytical_question: Does accepting occasional mistakes produce better total economics than tighter bureaucratic control?

retrieval_boundary.retrieves_when: Retrieve when decentralization creates visible mistakes and the analyst must judge the trade-off against cost, speed, and talent advantages.
retrieval_boundary.does_not_retrieve_when: Do not retrieve when errors reflect poor integrity or weak controls that threaten survival; use management_quality and risk principles.

performance_logic: Organizations trade off control against speed, cost, and talent attraction; accepting some mistakes can maximize long-run value when the overall system remains superior.
metric_definition: overhead_burden: cost of controls and central layers relative to enterprise value added
decision_cycle_time: speed of decisions under the organizational design
talent_density: concentration of capable people attracted and retained by the system
metric_usage_context: overhead_burden: use it to assess the hidden cost of designing for zero mistakes
decision_cycle_time: use it to judge whether control systems are slowing value creation
metric_limitations: control_failure_count: isolated mistakes can be overstressed relative to the broader economics of the system
overhead_burden: heavy control can appear prudent while reducing total expected value
evaluation_questions: Is management optimizing for control at the expense of initiative?
Are occasional errors an acceptable cost of a superior operating system?
Does the model raise overall expected value despite isolated mistakes?
diagnostic_signals: Lean structure with occasional errors but strong aggregate performance
Slow expensive bureaucracy designed to eliminate every mistake
practical_rules: Judge organizational systems by overall expected value, not by whether they eliminate all errors.
Accept some mistakes when tighter control would reduce speed, talent, and economics.

Added dynamic_regression_registry to preserve canonical and emergent Buffett concepts across future updates.

metadata_count_check.notes: Primary decision counts are computed from the 20-item 1980 merge_decision_log; final principle, sub-principle, and category counts are computed from framework_categories.

agent_usability_notes: Every top-level principle and sub-principle has a primary analytical question, retrieval aliases, decision use cases, and retrieval boundary.
1980's high-overlap items were mostly integrated into existing principle families to reduce future duplicate retrieval.
minor_issues: Source excerpts and chunk IDs remain unavailable in the supplied extraction JSONs, so evidence-grade traceability is limited to source years, item IDs, titles, and source-file references.
Two one-item categories were added, but both are canonical Buffett domains and are explicitly justified for future accumulation.
